title: 第 43 節
en: from similar blots. Many men were called atheists even then, not because they dreamt of denying the existence of a God, but because they wished to purify the idea of the Godhead from what seemed to them human exaggeration and human error.

In our own time we have learnt too well what atheism does mean, to use the word thus lightly and thoughtlessly. Yet it is well that whoever dares to be honest towards himself and towards others, be he layman or clergyman, should always remember what men they were who, before him, have been called blas phemers, heretics, or atheists.
There are moments in our life when those who seek most earnestly after God think they are for saken of God ; when they hardly venture to ask themselves, Do I then believe in God, or do I not ?
Let them not despair, and let us not judge harshly of them ; their despair may be better than many creeds.

Let me quote, in conclusion, the words of a great divine, lately deceased, whose honesty and piety have never been questioned. ' God' he says, ' is a great word. He who feels and understands that, will judge more mildly and more justly of those who confess that they dare not say that they believe in God.'

Now, I know perfectly well that what I have said just now will be misunderstood, will possibly be mis interpreted. I know I shall be accused of having defended and glorified atheism, and of having repre sented it as the last and highest point which man can reach in an evolution of religious thought. Let it be so ! If there are but a few here present who understand what I mean by honest atheism, and who HENOTHE1SM, POLYTHEISM, MONOTHEISM, ATHEISM. 309 know bow it differs from vulgar atheism, ay, from dishonest theism, I shall feel satisfied, for I know that to understand that distinction will often help us in the hour of our sorest need. It will teach us that, while the old leaves, the leaves of a bright and happy spring, are falling, and all seems wintry, frozen, and dead within and around us, there is and there must be a new spring in store for every warm and honest heart. It will teach us that honest doubt is the deepest spring of honest faith ; and that he only who has lost can find.

How the Indian mind, having arrived at this stage, grappled with this, the last and greatest of all religious problems, how it shook off, like another Laokoon, the coils of atheism, we shall see in our next and last lecture.
Collapse of the gods.

WHEN the Aryan settlers in India had arrived at the conviction that all their Devas or gods were mere names, we might imagine that they would have turned away in despair and disgust from what for ages they had adored and worshipped. Whether they had been deceived or had deceived themselves, the discovery that their old gods, their Indra, and Agrii, and Varu?ia, were names and nothing but names, was most likely to have produced on them the same impression as when the Greeks saw the temples of their gods demolished, or when the Germans stood by to see their sacred oaks felled, neither Apollo nor Odin appearing to avenge the sacrilege. But the result was totally different from what we should have expected. With the Greeks and Eomans and Germans we know that their ancient gods, when their course was run, disappeared either altogether, or, if their existence could not be entirely anni hilated, thev were degraded into evil and mischievous spirits ; while there was at the same time a new religion, namely Christianity, ready at hand, and capable of supplying those cravings of the heart which can never be entirely suppressed.

In India there was no such religion coming, as it were, from outside, in which the Brahmans, after they had lost their old gods and protectors, could have taken refuge. So, instead of turning aside and PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 311 making a new start, like the Greeks and Romans and Germans, they toiled on, on their own track, trusting that it would lead them right, if they fainted not in their search after what had been present to their minds from the first awakening of their senses, but what they had never been able to grasp firmly, to comprehend, or to name.

They threw away the old names, but they did not throw away their belief in that which they had tried to name. After destroying the altars of their old gods, they built out of the scattered bricks a new altar to the Unknown God — unknown, unnamed, and vet omnipresent ; seen no more in the mountains and rivers, in the sky and the sun, in the rain and the thunder, but present even then, and it may be, nearer to them, and encircling them, no longer like Varuna, the encircling and all-embracing ether, but more closely and more intimately, being, as they called it themselves, the very ether in their heart : it may be, the still small voice.
The object of divine appellation.

Let us remember, first, that the old poets of the Yeda did not say that Mitra, Varuna, and Agni were names and names only. They said l : ' They speak of Mitra, Varu/ia, Agni ; then he is the heavenly bird Garutmat ; that which is, and is one, the poets call in various ways ; they speak of Yama, Agni, Matarisvan.'
1 Rig- Veda, I, 164, 46, indram initram varu«ain agniin ahu/i atho divya/i sa/i suparaa/t garutman, ekam sat vipra/t bahudha vadanti, agnim yamam matarisvauum aim//.
Here then we see three things : first, that the poets never doubted that there was something real (sat), of which Agni, Indra, and Varuna, and all the rest, were but names.
Secondly, that that something real, was with them one, and one only.
Thirdly, that it must not be called one, as a mas culine, such as Pra//apati was, and other gods, but as a neuter.
Neuter names higher than masculine or feminine.

Now this, no doubt, jars on our ears. We cannot bear the neuter as a name of the divine. With us the neuter generally conveys the idea of something purely material, dead, or impersonal. But it was not so in ancient language, that is, in ancient thought ; it is not so even now in some of our modern languages. On the contrary, in choosing the neuter, the ancient sages tried to express something that should be neither male nor female, that should be in fact as far removed from weak human nature as weak human language could well express it ; some thing that should be higher than masculine or femi nine, not lower. They wanted a sex-less, by no means a life-less, or what some, without perceiving the con tradiction in terms, would call an impersonal God.

There are other passages where, though the poets speak of one God, with many names, they still speak of him in the masculine. Thus we read in a hymn addressed to the sun, and where the sun is likened to a bird ^ : ' Wise poets represent by their words 1 Eig-Veda, X, 114, 5, suparnam vipra/t kavayaA va&obhi/i ekam santam bahudha kalpayanti.
PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 313 the bird, who is one, in many ways/ This is to us pure mythology.
Less mythologically, but still very anthropomorphously, the supreme Being is represented in the following verse l :

' Who saw Him, when he was first born, when he who has no bones bore him who has bones 1 ' Where was the breath, the blood, the self of the world 1 Who went to ask this from any that knew itr Every one of these words is pregnant with thought. ' He who has no bones ' is an expression used to convey what we should express by saying, ' He who has no form ; ' while ' he who has bones ' is meant for that which has assumed consistency and form. 'The breath and blood of the world' again are attempts at expressing the unknown or invisible power, which supports the world. ' Breath ' is in fact the nearest approach to wrhat we should now call the essence or substance of the world.
Atman, the subjective Self.

This word, breath, in Sanskrit dtman, which is generally translated by self, is a word which, as we shall see, had a great future before it. Originally, it meant breath, then life, sometimes body; but far more frequently, the essence or the self. It became in fact a reflexive pronoun, like aJro?, ipse, or self. It was not, however, entirely restricted to this gram- I, 164, 4, ka/i dadarsa prathamam ^ayamanam asthanvantam yat anastha bibharti, bhumya/i asu7t asHk atma kva svit ka/i vidvamsam upa gat prash^um etat.

matical category, but entered upon a new career as the name of one of the highest philosophical ab stractions in India, or anywhere else. It was used to express, not simply the Ego or the I, for that Ego, the Aham, the I, was too much made up of the fleeting elements of this life. No, it expressed what was beyond the Ego, what supported the Ego for a time ; but, after a time, freed itself from the fetters and conditions of the human Ego, and became again the pure Self.

Atman differs from words which in other lan guages, after originally expressing breath, came to mean life, spirit, and soul. It lost its meaning of breath at a very early time, and after it had been divested of its physical meaning, after it had served as a mere pronoun, it became the vehicle of an abstraction more abstract even than ^fvxf) or TTvev/ma in Greek, anima or animus in Latin, asu or prawa in Sanskrit. In the Upanishads a belief in pra?ia, breath or spirit, as the true principle of existence, marks professedly a lower stage of philosophical knowledge than a belief in Atman, the Self. As with us the Self transcends the I, the Atman with the Hindus transcended the pra??a, and finally absorbed it.
This is the way in which, at a later time, the ancient Indian philosophers discovered the Infinite that supported their own being, the inward Self, as far beyond the Ego.
Atman, the objective Self.

Let us now see how they tried to discover the infinite in the outward or objective world. The poets had rested for a time in the One, PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 315 whom they conceived as the one god, but who was still masculine, active, slightly mythological ; who was in fact a divine Ego, not yet a divine Self. Suddenly, however, we light on passages of a different character. We seem to be moving in a new world. All that is dramatic and mytho logical, every form and every name, is surrendered, and there remains only ' the One/ or that which exists, as a neuter, as a last attempt to grasp the infinite.
zh: 從類似的汙點中，許多人甚至在當時就被稱為無神論者，並非因為他們夢想否認神的存在，而是因為他們希望將神性的觀念，從他們眼中看來屬於人類的誇大與謬誤中淨化出來。
在我們這個時代，我們已深切體會到無神論（atheism）的真正意涵，不會再如此輕率、不假思索地使用這個詞。然而，無論是平信徒還是神職人員，凡是敢於對自己與他人誠實的人，都應當永遠銘記：在他之前，那些曾被稱為褻瀆者、異端或無神論者的人，究竟是怎樣的人物。
在我們的生命中，有些時刻，那些最熱切尋求上帝的人，會以為自己被上帝所遺棄；在那樣的時刻，他們幾乎不敢自問：那麼，我究竟是信上帝，還是不信呢？
讓他們不要絕望，我們也不要嚴厲評斷他們；他們的絕望或許比許多信條更為可貴。
容我引述一位不久前辭世的偉大神學家的話語，其誠實與虔敬從未受人質疑。他說：「『上帝』是一個偉大的詞語。凡能感受並理解此點者，對於那些坦承自己不敢說信仰上帝的人，將會以更溫和、更公正的態度加以評斷。」

現在，我完全明白，我剛才所說的話將會被誤解，甚至可能被曲解。我知道自己將會被指控為辯護與頌揚無神論，並將其描繪為人類在宗教思想演進中所能達到的終極與最高點。但願如此！只要在座有少數人能夠理解我所謂「誠實的無神論」意指為何，並且知道它與粗俗的無神論——啊，甚至與不誠實的有神論——有何不同，我便心滿意足；因為我知道，理解這項區別，往往能在我們最為困頓的時刻提供幫助。它將教導我們：當舊葉——那明媚歡欣的春日之葉——紛紛飄落，當我們內外一切看似寒冬蕭瑟、冰封死寂之時，每一個溫暖而誠摯的心靈，都必然且終將迎來一個新的春天。它將教導我們：誠實的懷疑，乃是誠實信仰最深層的泉源；唯有曾經失去的人，才能夠真正尋獲。
印度心靈在抵達此一階段後，如何應對這最後且最偉大的宗教難題，又如何像另一位拉奧孔（Laokoon）般掙脫無神論的纏繞，我們將在下一講、亦即最後一講中看到。

然而，當我們從比較神話學轉向宗教學時，我們便從詩歌的領域進入了散文的領域。我們不再僅僅追蹤那些名稱的變遷，這些名稱曾一度被賦予無限者（the Infinite）的種種顯現，而後又在人類心靈的迷宮中迷失了方向。我們現在要問的是：這些名稱是如何獲得其權威的？它們是如何從隱喻蛻變為教條的？以及，最重要的是，它們是如何失去其力量的？因為諸神也會死亡；它們的崩潰與人類帝國的崩潰同樣真實，且往往更富悲劇性。一個民族可能被征服，其神廟可能被摧毀，但其神祇卻可能以另一種名義存活下來。然而，有時諸神會因一種更為致命的疾病而消亡——即「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。當人們不再理解那些古老名稱的原始意義時，當這些名稱從表達敬畏的詞語淪為空洞的聲音時，那麼，維繫諸神生命的信仰紐帶便斷裂了。於是，我們見證了諸神的崩潰——不是由於外敵的刀劍，而是由於內在的枯竭；不是由於更強大神祇的勝利，而是由於人類思想的覺醒，它開始追問那些曾經被視為理所當然的事物。

當印度的雅利安定居者終於確信，他們所有的提婆（Deva）或神祇都只是名稱時，我們或許會想像，他們會對那些長久以來所崇拜與敬奉的對象，感到絕望與厭惡而轉身離去。無論他們是被欺騙，還是自欺欺人，發現他們古老的神祇——因陀羅（Indra）、阿耆尼（Agni）與伐樓那（Varuṇa）——只是名稱，而且僅僅是名稱，這項發現極有可能在他們身上產生與希臘人目睹其神廟被摧毀、或日耳曼人旁觀其聖櫟被砍伐時相同的印象——無論是阿波羅（Apollo）還是奧丁（Odin）都未曾現身報復這褻瀆之舉。然而，結果卻與我們預期的截然不同。就希臘人、羅馬人與日耳曼人而言，我們知道，他們古老的神祇在氣數已盡之後，若非完全消失，便是——若其存在無法徹底湮滅——被貶為邪惡與為害的靈體；與此同時，卻有一門新的宗教，即基督教，近在咫尺，足以滿足那些永遠無法完全壓抑的內心渴求。
在印度，並未出現某種可謂從外部傳入的宗教，讓婆羅門在失去其古老諸神與庇護者之後得以棲身。因此，他們並未如希臘人、羅馬人與日耳曼人那樣轉向別途、另起爐灶，而是沿著自己的道路繼續奮力前行，堅信只要在追尋那自其感官初次甦醒便已縈繞心頭、卻始終未能牢牢掌握、理解或命名的對象時不喪失信心，這條路終將引領他們抵達正確的歸宿。

他們拋棄了舊有的名號，卻並未拋棄他們曾試圖命名的那個對象的信仰。在摧毀舊神祭壇之後，他們用散落的磚石為那未知之神築起一座新的祭壇——未知、無名，卻又無所不在；不再見於山川河流、蒼穹太陽、雨露雷電之中，但即使在那時，祂依然臨在，或許離他們更近，環繞著他們——不再像伐樓那（Varuna）那樣，是環繞並包容一切的以太，而是更緊密、更內在地臨在，正如他們自己所稱的，是他們心中的以太：或許，正是那微小的聲音。

在宗教學的早期階段，我們必須區分人們所崇拜的對象與他們用以稱呼這些對象的名稱。當我們說「神聖稱謂的對象」時，我們指的是那些被賦予神性之名的事物或存在——無論是自然現象、祖先靈魂，還是抽象概念。這些對象本身並非固定不變，而是隨著人類意識的發展而演變。例如，在吠陀（Veda）中，我們看到雅利安（Aryan）民族最初將自然力量如天空、太陽、風雨等視為神聖，並以特尤斯（Dyaus）、提婆（Deva）等名稱稱之。這些稱謂不僅反映了對無限者（the Infinite）的直覺，也揭示了語言如何塑造宗教觀念——正如穆勒所言，語言有時會成為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），導致神話與誤解。因此，研究神聖稱謂的對象，實為理解宗教學之鑰。
首先，讓我們記住，吠陀的古老詩人並未說密陀羅（Mitra）、伐樓那（Varuna）與阿耆尼（Agni）只是名稱而已。他們說道¹：「人們談論密陀羅、伐樓那、阿耆尼；然後祂是天鳥迦樓特曼（Garutmat）；那存在者，且是唯一者，詩人以各種方式稱呼；他們談論閻摩（Yama）、阿耆尼、摩多利首（Matarisvan）。」
他們稱祂為因陀羅、密多羅、伐樓那、阿耆尼，又或是那神聖的、翅翼華美的迦樓陀梵。智者們將那唯一之「有」以多種方式言說：稱之為阿耆尼、閻摩、摩多利首文。
在此，我們看見三件事：第一，詩人們從未懷疑存在某種真實之物（*sat*），而阿耆尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）、伐樓那（Varuna）以及其餘諸神，不過是此物的名稱。
其次，這個真實之物，對他們而言，是唯一且僅此唯一的。
第三，它不可被稱為「一」，如同補盧沙缽底（Prajāpati）及其他神祇那樣以陽性稱呼，而應以中性稱之。

在語言的深層結構中，中性名詞往往承載著比陽性或陰性名詞更為崇高的意涵。此現象不僅見諸梵文（*napuṃsaka*）與希臘文（*oudeteron*）的語法分類，更反映在宗教學對「無限者」（the Infinite）的表述方式上。當雅利安（Aryan）民族的早期文獻——如《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）——以中性詞「特尤斯」（Dyaus）指稱蒼穹時，其抽象性已超越陽性神祇「提婆」（Deva）的具體人格化特質。這種語言現象印證了穆勒（Müller）所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）如何影響人類對神聖的理解：從單一神教（henotheism）到輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的演進過程中，中性名詞的運用恰是比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的關鍵線索。

這無疑聽來刺耳。我們無法忍受以中性詞來稱呼神聖者。對我們而言，中性通常意味著某種純粹物質、死寂或非人格的東西。但在古代語言——亦即古代思想——中並非如此；即使在現代某些語言中，也並非如此。相反地，古代聖賢選擇中性詞，是試圖表達某種既非男性亦非女性、實際上應盡可能遠離脆弱人性的東西——正如脆弱的人類語言所能表達的那樣；某種應高於男性或女性、而非低於它們的東西。他們想要一個無性別的（sex-less），絕非無生命的（life-less），或某些人——未察覺其用詞矛盾——所謂的非人格神（impersonal God）。
> 還有其他段落，雖然詩人談論的是具有許多名字的單一神祇，他們仍以陽性來稱呼祂。例如，在一首獻給太陽的頌歌中，太陽被比作一隻鳥¹：「智者詩人以言語描繪¹梨俱吠陀，X，114，5，suparṇam viprāḥ kavayaḥ vacobhiḥ ekam santam bahudhā kalpayanti。」
哲學與宗教。313 那隻鳥，牠是一，以多種方式〔存在〕。對我們而言，這純粹是神話。
較少神話色彩，但仍極具擬人化地，至高存在在以下詩句中呈現¹：

「誰看見祂，當祂初生之時，當那無骨者生出那有骨者？¹ 世界的氣息、血液與自我何在？² 誰曾去向知者詢問此事？」³ 這些字句無一不蘊含深刻思想。「無骨者」一詞用以傳達我們今日所謂「無形者」之意；而「有骨者」則指已具備實質與形體之物。⁴ 再者，「世界的氣息與血液」是試圖表達那支撐世界、未知或不可見的力量。「氣息」實際上最接近我們現今所謂世界的本質或實體。⁵ --- ¹ 參見《梨俱吠陀》I, 164, 4；亦見《阿闥婆吠陀》X, 7, 21；X, 8, 2。 ² 參見《梨俱吠陀》I, 164, 4。 ³ 參見《梨俱吠陀》I, 164, 6。 ⁴ 參見穆勒《比較神話學》論文集，第二卷，第 112 頁。 ⁵ 參見《奧義書》中關於「氣息」（prāṇa）的討論。
Atman，即主體性的自我（Atman, the subjective Self）。
這個詞——梵文中的 *ātman*（通常譯為「自我」），正如我們將看到的，是一顆前途遠大的詞彙。它原意為「氣息」，而後指「生命」，有時也指「身體」；但更常見的是，它意指「本質」或「自我」。它實際上變成了一個反身代名詞，如同希臘文的 *αὐτός*、拉丁文的 *ipse*，或英文的 *self*。然而，它並未完全侷限於此一語法功能。
它原本屬於文法範疇，卻在印度——乃至任何地方——開啟了新的使命，成為最高哲學抽象概念之一的名稱。這個詞所表達的，並非單純的自我（Ego）或「我」（I），因為那個自我（Aham）、那個「我」，過度由此生短暫易逝的元素所構成。不，它所表達的是超越自我之物，是暫時支撐自我的東西；然而，經過一段時間後，它便掙脫了人類自我的束縛與條件，重新成為純粹的自我（Self）。

阿特曼（Atman）與其他語言中最初表達「氣息」、後來衍生為「生命」、「精神」與「靈魂」的詞彙有所不同。它在極早的時期便失去了「氣息」的含義；在剝除其物質意義、並被用作純粹的代名詞之後，它成為了一種抽象概念的載體——其抽象程度甚至超越希臘文的 ψυχή（psyche）或 πνεῦμα（pneuma）、拉丁文的 anima 或 animus，以及梵文的 asu 或 prāṇa。在《奧義書》中，相信 prāṇa（氣息或精神）為存在之真實原理，被明確標示為較相信阿特曼（Atman，即「自我」）更低階的哲學認知階段。正如在我們的文化中，「自我」（Self）超越了「我」（I），在印度教徒那裡，阿特曼（Atman）超越了 prāṇa，並最終將其吸納。
這是後來古印度哲學家發現支撐其自身存有之「無限者」的方式——內在的自我（Self），遠超乎個體自我（Ego）之上。

> 「在我們稱之為吠陀的古代讚美詩集中，我們發現了一個思想世界，它與我們自己的思想世界截然不同，卻又處處與之相連。我們在那裡看到一種宗教，它既非一神教，亦非多神教，而是我所謂的『單一神教（henotheism）』——一種對單一至高存在者的信仰，但此存在者尚未被提升到排他性的獨一神地位，而是被視為眾神之首，或眾神中最卓越者。然而，在這些讚美詩中，我們也發現了另一種宗教，它更為古老，更為原始——一種對無限者的感知，這種感知尚未被賦予任何名稱，卻已潛伏在人類心靈的深處，等待著被喚醒。」 **阿特曼（Ātman）**，即客觀的自我（the objective Self）。

現在讓我們看看他們如何試圖在外在或客觀世界中發現無限者。詩人們曾一度安歇於「獨一者」——*哲學與宗教* 315——他們將此獨一者構想為唯一的神，但祂仍是陽性、主動、略帶神話色彩的；事實上，祂是一個神聖的「自我」（Ego），而非一個神聖的「自性」（Self）。然而，我們突然間發現了性質迥異的段落。我們彷彿進入了一個全新的世界。所有戲劇性與神話性的元素、每一種形式與每一個名稱，都被揚棄了，只剩下「獨一者」，或作為中性詞的「存在者」（that which exists），作為把握無限者的最後嘗試。
